item.title: The Economics of AI and Employment: Why the Net Effect Is Not as Clear as It Seems
item.description:
Public concern about artificial intelligence displacing workers follows a pattern that is well-established in economic history. The power loom, the steam engine, the assembly line, and the personal computer each prompted similar predictions of widespread job loss. In each case, the technology did displace workers from specific tasks, but the aggregate employment consequences were more modest than feared, because cost reductions also expanded output and generated demand for labor in new and existing industries. Whether AI follows the same path is a genuinely open empirical question — but the analytical framework for thinking through it is straightforward, and it suggests considerably more ambiguity than most popular accounts acknowledge.

A General Production Framework

Consider a representative economy producing output using capital and labor :

where satisfies standard neoclassical properties: and . In competitive input markets, factors are paid their marginal products:

Equilibrium input demands satisfy .

AI as a Shift in Relative Marginal Products

AI can be modeled as a capital-augmenting technological shock that raises the effective productivity of capital relative to labor. Let parameterize the degree of AI adoption:

As increases, the marginal product of capital rises and the relative marginal product of labor falls:

This shift has two simultaneous effects: it lowers the relative cost of using capital, and it lowers the marginal cost of producing output. Both effects matter, and they pull labor demand in opposite directions.

The Output Response — and Why Measuring It Is Difficult

Because AI raises effective capital productivity, the minimum cost of producing any given falls. In a competitive product market, lower marginal cost pushes price toward the new, lower , and equilibrium output expands:

A complication is that standard GDP measurement likely understates this output expansion for AI. GDP captures market transactions at market prices, but a substantial share of AI’s value flows to users through free or heavily subsidized products. When producers use free AI tools to improve decision-making or operational efficiency, real economic value is created that does not appear in measured output. Economists have documented similar mismeasurement for digital goods more broadly; estimates suggest that free digital services generate consumer surplus worth hundreds of billions of dollars annually that conventional statistics miss (Brynjolfsson et al., 2019). The implication is that the true output effect of AI may be larger than GDP-based analyses would suggest, which strengthens the case that labor demand responses are being underestimated.

Decomposing the Labor Demand Response

The total effect on labor demand can be decomposed as:

The substitution effect is negative: holding output constant, the relative cheapening of effective capital induces firms to substitute toward and away from . This effect is real, but it represents only part of the full response.

The output effect is positive: AI-driven cost reductions expand equilibrium output , and since , this scale expansion pulls labor demand upward.

The net effect on employment is therefore theoretically indeterminate:

This is the honest answer that economic theory delivers. Whether AI increases or decreases aggregate employment is fundamentally an empirical question, not a theoretical one, and claims to the contrary — in either direction — are not well-grounded in economic reasoning.

Wages Are Equally Ambiguous

The same decomposition logic applies to wages. The wage effect depends on how AI changes the marginal product of labor. With capital-augmenting AI, the direction turns on whether capital and labor are gross complements or gross substitutes:

  • If and are gross complements (), expanding effective capital raises , putting upward pressure on wages even as relative input prices shift toward capital.
  • If and are gross substitutes (), AI-driven capital deepening depresses , pushing wages down.

Most empirical estimates of the aggregate elasticity of substitution between capital and labor fall in the range , suggesting mild gross complementarity (Chirinko, 2008). If this holds, AI adoption could raise average wages even while displacing workers from specific tasks. The more empirically grounded near-term concern is distributional rather than aggregate: automation tends to compress wages in routine-task occupations while raising returns to high-skill and manual-dexterity roles (Autor, Levy, and Murnane, 2003). The question, accordingly, may be less about total employment than about uneven adjustment across workers and occupations.

The Elasticity Condition

The indeterminacy can be sharpened with a precise condition. Labor demand falls if and only if:

where is the elasticity of substitution between and , is the own-price elasticity of product demand, and is labor’s share of total cost. When product demand is highly elastic (large ), output expands substantially and the output effect tends to dominate. When factor substitution is easy (large ), employment may fall even as output grows.

The aggregate unemployment scenario consequently requires either very large or very small — the latter being equivalent to assuming that output barely responds to substantially lower production costs.

The Historical Record

The empirical record from comparable general-purpose technologies is informative, though not definitive.

Agricultural mechanization in the 19th and early 20th centuries displaced the large majority of the agricultural workforce in developed economies. In 1800, roughly 90% of the American labor force worked in agriculture; today the figure is less than 2%. Total employment did not decline; labor reallocated into manufacturing and services, with real wages rising over the same period.

Electrification in the early 20th century is perhaps the closest historical analogue to AI: a general-purpose technology that augmented capital productivity across virtually every sector simultaneously. Factories reorganized around the new technology, displacing many craft workers, yet the decades following broad electrification saw robust wage growth in manufacturing, as productivity gains raised the marginal product of the remaining workers and created new consumer goods industries (David, 1990).

The computing and internet revolution produced similar concerns in the 1980s and 1990s. Automated teller machines were widely cited as evidence that bank tellers would become obsolete — but the number of bank tellers in the United States increased in the decades following ATM adoption, because lower branch operating costs enabled banks to open more locations and expand services (Bessen, 2015). Aggregate employment in IT-intensive industries grew substantially through the 1990s and 2000s even as individual tasks were automated.

The pattern across these episodes is consistent enough that economic historians treat it as a near-regularity: general-purpose technologies that substantially lower production costs have historically generated more aggregate labor demand, not less, after full adjustment. The adjustment process is not without cost — workers in displaced occupations bear real transition costs — but the aggregate employment declines that were predicted did not materialize.

A More Complete Accounting

Analyses that conclude AI will reduce aggregate employment generally focus exclusively on the substitution effect, implicitly assuming that output responds minimally to lower costs. A more complete accounting must incorporate the output response, the reallocation of labor toward tasks where human comparative advantage persists, and the likelihood that AI’s true contribution to output is being understated in standard statistics.

None of this implies that the adjustment to AI will be painless or evenly distributed. The distributional consequences of automation are real and warrant serious policy attention. However, aggregate employment outcomes depend on both the substitution and output effects, and the weight of historical evidence suggests caution about confident predictions of labor market catastrophe from general-purpose technological change.

References

Autor, D. H., Levy, F., and Murnane, R. J. (2003). The skill content of recent technological change: An empirical exploration. Quarterly Journal of Economics, 118(4), 1279–1333.

Bessen, J. (2015). Learning by Doing: The Real Connection Between Innovation, Wages, and Wealth. Yale University Press.

Brynjolfsson, E., Eggers, F., and Gannamaneni, A. (2019). Using massive online choice experiments to measure changes in well-being. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 116(15), 7250–7255.

Chirinko, R. S. (2008). σ: The long and short of it. Journal of Macroeconomics, 30(2), 671–686.

David, P. A. (1990). The dynamo and the computer: An historical perspective on the modern productivity paradox. American Economic Review, 80(2), 355–361.

item.title: What Happens to Feedlot Performance When Feed Prices Shift?
item.description:
Feed costs typically account for 60–70% of the total cost of gaining a pound of beef in a feedlot, which makes feedlot managers acutely sensitive to prices for corn, distillers grains, hay, and the many other ingredients that flow in and out of a finishing ration. While the relationship between feed costs and profitability is well-established in the literature, the mechanism through which feed prices affect cattle performance is less understood. In a study I recently published with co-authors James Mitchell and Jaime Luke in the Journal of Agricultural and Applied Economics, we examine that mechanism directly.

The key insight motivating the study is that when feed prices shift, profit-maximizing feedlot managers don’t simply absorb the cost change — they adjust. They substitute one ingredient for another in response to changing relative prices, and those substitution decisions alter the composition of the ration, which in turn affects how efficiently cattle convert feed to gain. Accordingly, higher feed prices don’t translate mechanically into worse performance; the outcome depends on what ingredients are substituted and how those substitutes perform on the margin.

Data and Measures of Performance

We used nearly 30 years of monthly closeout data from the Kansas Focus on Feedlots survey — a well-established benchmark for feedlot performance in the Southern Plains — spanning April 1995 through September 2024. We focused on three measures of technical efficiency that feedlot managers track closely: the feed conversion ratio (FCR), average daily gain (ADG), and days on feed (DOF).

The feed conversion ratio measures pounds of feed required per pound of gain, where lower values indicate greater efficiency. Average daily gain measures pounds gained per day, with higher values indicating better performance. Days on feed measures the length of the feeding period, where shorter periods are generally preferred, all else equal. These three measures capture complementary dimensions of feedlot performance and allow us to evaluate how feed price changes affect efficiency through multiple channels.

Separating the Effects of Price Changes

A central contribution of this study is the separation of feed price effects into distinct components. The total effect of a price change on efficiency includes all the ways a feedlot might respond: adjusting ration composition, changing placement weights, and changing slaughter weights. To isolate what is specifically attributable to feed ingredient substitution — independent of changes in cattle weights — we estimated additional model specifications that hold placement weight and slaughter weight constant. The consistent finding across specifications is that feed substitution is the dominant pathway through which price changes affect technical efficiency. It is primarily about what cattle are switched to, not simply whether they are fed more or less.

Key Findings by Ingredient

Results varied considerably across feed ingredients, and several findings were contrary to what one might expect.

Alfalfa price increases were associated with improved FCR and faster average daily gain. Alfalfa is not only a roughage but also a meaningful source of protein and energy, and when its price rises and feedlots substitute away from it, the replacement feeds tend to maintain or improve the diet’s energy and protein density. The result is improved performance on the margin.

Dried distillers grains (DDGs) produced one of the most notable findings from a management standpoint. Increases in DDG prices were associated with significantly more days on feed and slower average daily gain across all model specifications. DDGs are a high-energy, high-protein ethanol by-product that feedlots rely on heavily. When they become relatively more expensive and feedlots substitute to lower-energy alternatives, cattle performance declines measurably. This result underscores the importance of DDGs as a key ingredient for achieving efficient weight gain.

Sorghum and barley price increases were consistently associated with worse FCR and slower gains, suggesting that substitutes for these grains at the margin are less efficient. Conversely, higher soymeal and wheat prices were associated with improved FCR, suggesting that when feedlots substitute away from these ingredients, the replacements are performing more efficiently on the margin.

Corn price changes showed no statistically significant association with FCR, ADG, or days on feed. This is a notable finding given corn’s central role in feedlot rations. Two explanations are plausible. First, demand for corn as a feedlot ingredient may be relatively price-inelastic — feedlots may not reduce corn inclusion substantially even when prices rise, because no close substitute performs as well. Second, when feedlots do substitute away from corn, they tend to use grains with similar nutritional profiles, such as sorghum, and the performance effects largely offset.

Implications for Management and Policy

For feedlot managers, these results offer a clearer picture of what to expect when specific market prices move. Not all feed price increases carry equivalent performance consequences. A spike in DDG prices, for instance, carries real efficiency costs if managers substitute to lower-energy alternatives, whereas increases in alfalfa prices may push the ration toward more efficient substitutes. The efficiency effects estimated here are the result of profit-maximizing decisions already being made by feedlot managers; consequently, this research is not prescriptive. Rather, it provides information that may be useful for planning and logistics as market conditions change.

There are also policy implications to consider. Commodity prices are affected by programs such as the Renewable Fuel Standard, which influences both corn and DDG prices through its effects on ethanol demand. If such policies alter the relative price of DDGs and lead feedlots to substitute toward lower-energy ingredients, the effects on days on feed could translate into changes in total greenhouse gas emissions from fed cattle, given that emissions over a steer’s lifetime are influenced by the length of the feeding period. Our results provide estimates that could be used to evaluate these kinds of unintended consequences in future policy analyses.

Summary

Feed prices affect feedlot technical efficiency primarily through the substitution decisions they trigger, rather than through cost effects alone. The direction and magnitude of those effects vary by ingredient, and in some cases run counter to intuition. DDGs stand out as particularly important for maintaining performance, while corn price changes appear to have little detectable effect on efficiency outcomes. These findings have implications for how feedlot managers interpret market signals and for the evaluation of commodity-related policies that may indirectly affect the cattle feeding sector.

This post summarizes findings from: Anderson, A.E., J.L. Mitchell, and J.R. Luke. 2026. “Feed Prices, Substitution Patterns, and Technical Efficiency in Feedlot Cattle.” Journal of Agricultural and Applied Economics 58(1):17–37. https://doi.org/10.1017/aae.2025.10018
